verses.text: Now Joab the son of Zeruiah perceived that the king's heart was toward Absalom. Guidance: Joab reads David. The verb 'perceived' (yada, knew) implies careful observation over time — Joab has been watching the king grieve for Amnon, then long for Absalom (the closing words of chapter 13: 'the soul of king David longed to go forth unto Absalom'). The king has not said anything directly, but his commander sees it. Joab's perception sets up the chapter's central political maneuver. Joab is not a moral guide; he is a strategic operator. He sees that David's emotional state is creating instability at court — a king who privately longs for an exiled son but publicly maintains exile is a king whose heart and policy are at war. Joab decides to resolve the tension by engineering Absalom's return. The motive is partly political (a divided king is dangerous), partly personal (Joab has affection for Absalom, the cousin's son), partly opportunistic (Joab will accumulate political capital by being the agent of reconciliation). The chapter is going to show us what happens when the king's private emotion is moved by indirect means rather than direct conversation. The reconciliation Joab engineers will be incomplete; it will set up the rebellion of chapter 15. Sometimes the wisest courtiers see real problems and propose flawed solutions.

And Joab sent to Tekoah, and fetched thence a wise woman, and said unto her, I pray thee, feign thyself to be a mourner, and put on now mourning apparel, and anoint not thyself with oil, but be as a woman that had a long time mourned for the dead: And come to the king, and speak on this manner unto him. So Joab put the words in her mouth. Guidance: Joab's strategy: produce a parable, like Nathan's in chapter 12, that will catch David in his own verdict. Nathan's parable worked when David condemned the rich man's theft of the lamb; Joab will design a parallel parable to move David to recall Absalom. He sends to Tekoa (a small town about ten miles south of Jerusalem, the future hometown of the prophet Amos) for 'a wise woman.' Tekoa apparently had a reputation for women skilled in wisdom-literature performance — the kind of practiced rhetorical talent we would call a court-orator today. Joab coaches her: dress as a mourner, no anointing oil (the sign of festivity), present yourself as long-bereaved. 'Joab put the words in her mouth.' He scripts every line. The strategy is risky in a particular way — David has been fooled by a prophetic parable before; he might catch the device this time. The chapter is going to show that David does catch the device, but accepts the lesson anyway. Wise leadership sometimes accepts the message even when it sees through the messenger's scripted delivery.

And when the woman of Tekoah spake to the king, she fell on her face to the ground, and did obeisance, and said, Help, O king. And the king said unto her, What aileth thee? And she answered, I am indeed a widow woman, and mine husband is dead. And thy handmaid had two sons, and they two strove together in the field, and there was none to part them, but the one smote the other, and slew him. And, behold, the whole family is risen against thine handmaid, and they said, Deliver him that smote his brother, that we may kill him, for the life of his brother whom he slew; and we will destroy the heir also: and so they shall quench my coal which is left, and shall not leave to my husband neither name nor remainder upon the earth. Guidance: The parable. The woman prostrates and asks for help. David asks the standard kingly question: 'what aileth thee?' She tells the story Joab gave her: she is a widow; her two sons fought in a field; one killed the other; the family is now demanding the surviving son be executed as the murderer; if the family succeeds, her husband's name dies out (no male heir to carry the name forward), her dynastic 'coal' (Hebrew gachelet, the last remaining ember of a fire — a tender metaphor for the last surviving son who keeps the family line burning) is quenched. The parable is structured to highlight the conflict between blood-vengeance law (the family's claim, grounded in Num 35:19) and dynastic continuity (the widow's claim, grounded in the LORD's care for inheritance and lineage in passages like Ruth and Deut 25:5-10). The parable's parallel to David is precise: David has one son (Amnon) dead; another son (Absalom) under sentence-by-default for the killing; if Absalom dies, David's family of contenders is decimated. The chapter is showing us Joab's rhetorical craft. The parable, like Nathan's, works on the conscience by reframing what is happening in David's house through someone else's family. David hears it and recognizes the same instinct that produced the Bathsheba-Nathan trap, but the chapter will show him receiving it anyway.

And the king said unto the woman, Go to thine house, and I will give charge concerning thee. And the woman of Tekoah said unto the king, My lord, O king, the iniquity be on me, and on my father's house: and the king and his throne be guiltless. And the king said, Whosoever saith ought unto thee, bring him to me, and he shall not touch thee any more. Then said she, I pray thee, let the king remember the LORD thy God, that thou wouldest not suffer the revengers of blood to destroy any more, lest they destroy my son. And he said, As the LORD liveth, there shall not one hair of thy son fall to the earth. Guidance: David escalates his protection in three stages. (1) 'Go to thine house, and I will give charge concerning thee' — a general promise. (2) 'Whosoever saith ought unto thee, bring him to me, and he shall not touch thee any more' — specific protection from family pressure. (3) Finally the oath: 'As the LORD liveth, there shall not one hair of thy son fall to the earth.' Capital protection by royal oath. The widow has worked David up to a full commitment that the surviving son will not be executed, regardless of the blood-vengeance law that would otherwise apply. She has used the language carefully — 'let the king remember the LORD thy God' (invoking God), 'the revengers of blood' (naming the avengers' role), 'destroy my son' (personalizing). David, who in chapter 13 could not act to protect Tamar or punish Amnon or restrain Absalom, now finds himself capable of fierce, immediate protection of a peasant widow's son he has never met. The chapter is showing us that David's compassion is intact for cases outside his family; it is his own family where the moral paralysis lives.

Then the woman said, Let thine handmaid, I pray thee, speak one word unto my lord the king. And he said, Say on. And the woman said, Wherefore then hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God? for the king doth speak this thing as one which is faulty, in that the king doth not fetch home again his banished. For we must needs die, and are as water spilt on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again; neither doth God respect any person: yet doth he devise means, that his banished be not expelled from him. Guidance: The widow turns the case. Having got the king's commitment, she now applies the principle. 'Wherefore then hast thou thought such a thing against the people of God?' She names the contradiction: the king is willing to protect her son but is unwilling to do the same for the entire nation's interest in not losing the king's own son to permanent exile. 'For we must needs die, and are as water spilt on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again.' One of the great lines of biblical anthropology — death is irrevocable; nothing you do can bring back the dead. Amnon cannot be brought back; that is settled. But: 'neither doth God respect any person: yet doth he devise means, that his banished be not expelled from him.' The verse is theologically extraordinary. The widow is teaching David the LORD's own pattern: God himself 'devises means' (chashav machashavot, 'thinks thoughts' — i.e., plans deliberately) so that those banished by sin do not stay banished forever. The reference is to God's covenant pattern of bringing exiles back, both literally and morally. The widow is suggesting that what David could do for Absalom would be a small reflection of what the LORD does for all of His banished people. The verse becomes one of the great Old Testament hints of redemptive theology: God devises means that His banished be not expelled from Him. The verse hangs over the rest of biblical theology as a description of what redemption is — the LORD figuring out how to bring His exiled people home. The widow's parable is now over; she has applied it to David's situation; the lesson has landed.

Now therefore that I am come to speak of this thing unto my lord the king, it is because the people have made me afraid: and thy handmaid said, I will now speak unto the king; it may be that the king will perform the request of his handmaid. For the king will hear, to deliver his handmaid out of the hand of the man that would destroy me and my son together out of the inheritance of God. Then thine handmaid said, The word of my lord the king shall now be comfortable: for as an angel of God, so is my lord the king to discern good and bad: therefore the LORD thy God will be with thee. Guidance: The widow continues with her cover story. She tells David that her motive in coming was fear of the family pressure, and her hope that he would deliver her from those who would 'destroy me and my son together out of the inheritance of God.' The phrase 'inheritance of God' (nachalat elohim) is striking — the Israelite tribal land allotment is named as the LORD's inheritance, and the widow's son's loss would mean a piece of God's people permanently disappears. She is making her parable-domain (peasant family) carry the same theological weight as the kingdom-domain (David's family). Then the praise: 'as an angel of God, so is my lord the king to discern good and bad.' She places David in the angelic category of moral discernment. The flattery is partly genuine (David is, after all, the LORD's anointed) and partly strategic (it sets him up to accept the verdict she is about to suggest he draw himself). 'The LORD thy God will be with thee' — the closing blessing. The widow has now finished her courtroom performance.

Then the king answered and said unto the woman, Hide not from me, I pray thee, the thing that I shall ask thee. And the woman said, Let my lord the king now speak. And the king said, Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this? And the woman answered and said, As thy soul liveth, my lord the king, none can turn to the right hand or to the left from ought that my lord the king hath spoken: for thy servant Joab, he bade me, and he put all these words in the mouth of thine handmaid: To fetch about this form of speech hath thy servant Joab done this thing: and my lord is wise, according to the wisdom of an angel of God, to know all things that are in the earth. Guidance: David sees through the device. 'Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this?' He has been listening to the parable while quietly recognizing that it was professionally crafted, and he has been mapping the structure backward to its likely author. Joab is the only courtier with the relationship and motive to commission such a piece. David asks. The woman confirms instantly: 'Thy servant Joab, he bade me, and he put all these words in the mouth of thine handmaid: to fetch about this form of speech hath thy servant Joab done this thing.' She does not lie further. The chapter is showing us a king who, even in his moral paralysis, has not lost his strategic perception. He can spot a coached witness. The widow's flattery — 'my lord is wise, according to the wisdom of an angel of God' — is more sincere now; she is praising the king for catching the device. The chapter is teaching us that wise leadership can both see through manipulation and accept the truth the manipulation was carrying. David could have been angry at Joab's stunt; instead he is going to take the underlying lesson. There is a category of leader who values content over delivery — who can receive the correct word even when its packaging is artificial.

Then the king answered and said unto the woman, Hide not from me, I pray thee, the thing that I shall ask thee. And the woman said, Let my lord the king now speak. And the king said, Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this? And the woman answered and said, As thy soul liveth, my lord the king, none can turn to the right hand or to the left from ought that my lord the king hath spoken: for thy servant Joab, he bade me, and he put all these words in the mouth of thine handmaid: To fetch about this form of speech hath thy servant Joab done this thing: and my lord is wise, according to the wisdom of an angel of God, to know all things that are in the earth. Guidance: David sees through the device. 'Is not the hand of Joab with thee in all this?' He has been listening to the parable while quietly recognizing that it was professionally crafted, and he has been mapping the structure backward to its likely author. Joab is the only courtier with the relationship and motive to commission such a piece. David asks. The woman confirms instantly: 'Thy servant Joab, he bade me, and he put all these words in the mouth of thine handmaid: to fetch about this form of speech hath thy servant Joab done this thing.' She does not lie further. The chapter is showing us a king who, even in his moral paralysis, has not lost his strategic perception. He can spot a coached witness. The widow's flattery — 'my lord is wise, according to the wisdom of an angel of God' — is more sincere now; she is praising the king for catching the device. The chapter is teaching us that wise leadership can both see through manipulation and accept the truth the manipulation was carrying. David could have been angry at Joab's stunt; instead he is going to take the underlying lesson. There is a category of leader who values content over delivery — who can receive the correct word even when its packaging is artificial.

And the king said unto Joab, Behold now, I have done this thing: go therefore, bring the young man Absalom again. And Joab fell to the ground on his face, and bowed himself, and thanked the king: and Joab said, To day thy servant knoweth that I have found grace in thy sight, my lord, O king, in that the king hath fulfilled the request of his servant. Guidance: David accepts the lesson. He summons Joab and commands the recall: 'Behold now, I have done this thing: go therefore, bring the young man Absalom again.' Joab prostrates and thanks the king. Note the language Joab uses: 'I have found grace in thy sight… the king hath fulfilled the request of his servant.' Joab now openly owns the manipulation, no longer pretending it was the widow's initiative. He thanks David for accepting the message. The chapter is showing us the courtly transaction: Joab pushed; David received; the recall is ordered. But the chapter is going to immediately reveal a fatal flaw in David's acceptance — he has agreed to bring Absalom home, but he has not agreed to forgive him. The recall will not be a full restoration; it will be a partial reconciliation that produces the worst of both outcomes.

And Joab fell to the ground on his face, and bowed himself, and thanked the king: and Joab said, To day thy servant knoweth that I have found grace in thy sight, my lord, O king, in that the king hath fulfilled the request of his servant. The recall will not be a full restoration; it will be a partial reconciliation that produces the worst of both outcomes.

So Joab arose and went to Geshur, and brought Absalom to Jerusalem. And the king said, Let him turn to his own house, and let him not see my face. So Absalom returned to his own house, and saw not the king's face. Guidance: Joab fetches Absalom from Geshur and brings him to Jerusalem. The half-reconciliation: 'Let him turn to his own house, and let him not see my face.' Absalom is allowed back inside the borders of Israel, the city of Jerusalem, and his own former residence — but he is not allowed into the king's presence. The Hebrew 'see my face' means face-to-face audience, the political and personal recognition that would constitute full reinstatement to royal-son status. David has done half of what Joab wanted. He has done the visible-recall while withholding the relational-restoration. The chapter is showing us the precise terms of a half-measure. From David's perspective, this is justice-with-mercy: Amnon's killer is alive, but the king is not pretending the killing did not happen. From Absalom's perspective, this is the worst possible result — he is back in the city of his ambitions, watching the throne, but excluded from the relationship that would have healed him. The half-measure is going to produce, over the next four chapters, a fully-developed rebellion. The chapter is teaching us that partial reconciliation can be worse than no reconciliation. It restores the geography of contact while denying the substance of restoration. The unforgiven son in proximity to the unforgiving father will, over the next two years, ferment into a rival king.

But in all Israel there was none to be so much praised as Absalom for his beauty: from the sole of his foot even to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him. And when he polled his head, (for it was at every year's end that he polled it: because the hair was heavy on him, therefore he polled it:) he weighed the hair of his head at two hundred shekels after the king's weight. And unto Absalom there were born three sons, and one daughter, whose name was Tamar: she was a woman of a fair countenance. Guidance: The narrator pauses for a portrait of Absalom. 'In all Israel there was none to be so much praised as Absalom for his beauty: from the sole of his foot even to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him.' Absalom is the kingdom's most beautiful man — the same description-pattern earlier used to praise Saul (1 Sam 9:2) and David (1 Sam 16:12). The chapter is signaling that Absalom has the physical attributes of a king. Then the famous detail about his hair: he cuts it once a year because it is so heavy, and the cuttings weigh '200 shekels after the king's weight' — about five pounds (the 'king's weight' was an official standard, slightly heavier than the common shekel). The detail is striking. The Old Testament does not normally pause on hair-weight; the narrator is foregrounding it because the hair will become a central image in chapter 18, when Absalom's hair gets caught in the branches of an oak and the king's army kills him. The chapter is foreshadowing the death-by-vanity. Then the family: three sons (unnamed, and 18:18 will tell us they all died young; Absalom set up a stone pillar instead because he had no sons to keep his name) and one daughter named Tamar — named for his violated sister, in unhealed memory of the original wound that produced everything that followed. The chapter is showing us a man defined by his outward beauty, his vanity-grade hair, and a single daughter named in honor of the sister he could not forget. The portrait is sympathetic and tragic at once.

So Absalom dwelt two full years in Jerusalem, and saw not the king's face. Therefore Absalom sent for Joab, to have sent him to the king; but he would not come to him: and when he sent again the second time, he would not come. Guidance: 'Two full years.' Total time of the half-exile: Absalom in Jerusalem but not at court. Two years are added to the three years he had been in Geshur (13:38) — five years total of estrangement. Absalom finally cannot bear it. He sends for Joab to intercede with the king for a face-to-face audience. Joab refuses. Sends again; Joab refuses again. The man who engineered Absalom's recall is now refusing to engineer his full restoration. Joab probably calculates that his political capital with David is finite; pushing on the full reconciliation will expend it. Or he thinks the half-measure is the wisest possible outcome — David is content with the status quo and would react badly to renewed pressure. Either way, Joab will not move. Absalom realizes that polite channels will not work. The chapter is going to show his next move, which is going to be characteristic of the angry-prince personality the rebellion will reveal.

Or he thinks the half-measure is the wisest possible outcome — David is content with the status quo and would react badly to renewed pressure. Either way, Joab will not move. Absalom realizes that polite channels will not work. The chapter is going to show his next move, which is going to be characteristic of the angry-prince personality the rebellion will reveal.

Therefore he said unto his servants, See, Joab's field is near mine, and he hath barley there; go and set it on fire. And Absalom's servants set the field on fire. Guidance: Absalom orders his servants to burn down Joab's barley field. The act is a deliberate provocation — destruction of agricultural property forces a face-to-face confrontation. Absalom has effectively decided that, since polite requests have failed, he will commit a property crime serious enough to force Joab to come ask him to stop. The strategy is morally ugly (collateral economic damage to Joab) but politically effective (Joab cannot ignore the burned crop). The chapter is showing us Absalom's character. He gets what he wants by destroying the things of people who frustrate him. The same instinct will be in play when he later launches the rebellion: when the kingdom is not arranged to his liking, he is willing to set the country on fire to force a meeting. The hair-vanity foreshadowing and the barley-arson here both point to the man Absalom is becoming.

Then Joab arose, and came to Absalom unto his house, and said unto him, Wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire? And Absalom answered Joab, Behold, I sent unto thee, saying, Come hither, that I may send thee to the king, to say, Wherefore am I come from Geshur? it had been good for me to have been there still: now therefore let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me. Guidance: Joab comes — exactly as Absalom calculated. 'Wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire?' Absalom's answer is unapologetic. 'I sent unto thee, saying, Come hither.' He has reframed the arson as a delivery service. Then the substantive complaint: 'Wherefore am I come from Geshur? It had been good for me to have been there still.' The half-recall has been worse than the exile; in Geshur he was at least the favored grandson of the king of an Aramean kingdom; in Jerusalem he is the unwelcome son of his own father. The demand: 'Let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me.' Absalom asks for a decision. He wants either full restoration or full execution. The half-state is intolerable. The request is, on its face, dignified — better death than indefinite estrangement. It is also slightly menacing. Absalom is forcing David's hand. The chapter is making the next move David's.

Then Joab arose, and came to Absalom unto his house, and said unto him, Wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire? And Absalom answered Joab, Behold, I sent unto thee, saying, Come hither, that I may send thee to the king, to say, Wherefore am I come from Geshur? it had been good for me to have been there still: now therefore let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me. Guidance: Joab comes — exactly as Absalom calculated. 'Wherefore have thy servants set my field on fire?' Absalom's answer is unapologetic. 'I sent unto thee, saying, Come hither.' He has reframed the arson as a delivery service. Then the substantive complaint: 'Wherefore am I come from Geshur? It had been good for me to have been there still.' The half-recall has been worse than the exile; in Geshur he was at least the favored grandson of the king of an Aramean kingdom; in Jerusalem he is the unwelcome son of his own father. The demand: 'Let me see the king's face; and if there be any iniquity in me, let him kill me.' Absalom asks for a decision. He wants either full restoration or full execution. The half-state is intolerable. The request is, on its face, dignified — better death than indefinite estrangement. It is also slightly menacing. Absalom is forcing David's hand. The chapter is making the next move David's.

So Joab came to the king, and told him: and when he had called for Absalom, he came to the king, and bowed himself on his face to the ground before the king: and the king kissed Absalom. Guidance: Joab relays the message. David, faced with the choice, summons Absalom. The reception scene: 'when he had called for Absalom, he came to the king, and bowed himself on his face to the ground before the king: and the king kissed Absalom.' The kiss is the formal sign of full reconciliation — the same gesture Esau used to receive Jacob (Gen 33:4), the father in the prodigal-son parable will use, and the standard ancient Near-Eastern royal-restoration ceremony. David has now, after five years (three of exile, two of Jerusalem residence without audience), publicly restored his son. The chapter ends with the visible reconciliation accomplished. But the chapter is also showing us the cost of how it was accomplished. (1) It came after five years, not immediately. (2) It required Joab's manipulation, then Absalom's arson, then Joab's pressure — none of the three the kind of approach to David that would have been possible if David had been emotionally healthy. (3) The kiss is the formal closure of the matter, but the chapter does not say David has truly forgiven, and we will see in the next chapter that Absalom has not been spiritually healed by the gesture. The kiss looks like resolution; the next chapter will show it was only ceremony. Tomorrow, chapter 15 will open with Absalom buying chariots and horsemen and standing at the city gate stealing the hearts of the men of Israel. The chapter has not produced a reconciled son; it has produced a son who knows how to manipulate his way back into the city, and is about to use the same instinct to manipulate his way to the throne. The chapter is one of the most psychologically realistic in 2 Samuel about how unhealed family wounds, papered over by formal gestures, do not actually heal. The kiss closes the chapter; the rebellion is in the very next paragraph.
